# Ganesh Mani (AI and Emerging Economies)

<!-- vimeo: 1111493458 | track: English (auto-generated) -->

[▶ Watch on Vimeo](https://vimeo.com/1111493458)

<details>
<summary>자막: Ganesh Mani (AI and Emerging Economies)</summary>

Hello, I'm Ganesh Money faculty at Carnegie Mellon. Last year I served as the inaugural director of collaborative AI at the Tepper School of Business. Today I share how we are preparing students for the future of global work through experiential learning and human machine teaming. We call it Practicing Management Science 2.0. Across CMU global campuses, I teach a wide variety of project-based courses. The focus is always on learning by doing. Increasingly, we're exploring how to collaborate effectively and multi human, multi machine teams, which is the future. During the spring semester of 2025, we had 167 students working on 39 MSBA Master of Science and Business Analytics Capstone projects. These projects connected students with real world challenges from companies like Honda, Maersk, and Cron. Events like the Data Nexus poster session at the end help students showcase their work, refining their communication skills, and also getting recruited. One of my favorite courses is the AI and Emerging Economies course that I teach in the fall via Zoom to students across Africa, Pittsburgh, and Qatar. It's a multidisciplinary experience. Students work in global teams apply tools like Chat, GPT, and tackle case studies. It builds critical and creative thinking in them. You may have heard of white coding very similarly. We've built a web optimization framework using OpenAI and Pal. OpenAI helps extract the skill requirements from the Capstone project descriptions. The framework then allocates students to Capstone teams based on their preferences and their self-reported skills. So it aligns skills with actually what the project demands. Solving a mixed inte linear program with LLM guidance and we're experimenting extensively with collective intelligence platforms like Think Scape from unanimous start, AI GPT-4 Oh and SWAN Intelligence Principles underlie the platform. Students deliberate and converge on decisions like asset allocation tailored to certain personas. It's a very powerful way to demonstrate reasoning, consensus building and decision making, and how emerging tools make all this more efficient. We've also designed customizable case studies like evaluating an AI data center investment in Columbia, an emerging economy. Students conduct net present value and IRR analysis with and without Chad GPT and reflect on financial and non-financial risks. For example, geopolitical risk. These cases reinforce fundamental thinking and real world application. OpenAI allows us to create bespoke cases at scale. Our creative students are building great prototypes, a macroeconomic simulation using agents as part of an independent study. This was run by a second year then undergrad, a landro powered research assistant developed during a team. Capstone Landro is an agent at framework from my colleague Dr. Ani. The digital research assistant decomposes complex questions, searches academic resources, and generates structured reports. This is a glimpse into the future of agent-based learning and research. We've seen measurable impact in trials comparing gene infused platforms like scape to traditional methods like whiteboard brainstorming, which doesn't work well for large groups. Students reported more efficient decision making. They also said it improved their financial literacy. We poured them. This is more of a side effect. One Capstone team partnered with Toys electronics to analyze and improve gamified steam education. They built AI driven tools for career guidance and mentorship using Azure OpenAI components. Our work is also influencing programs like the tia.org, young Entrepreneurs Summer School, which is partly hosted at the Schwartz Center. Here at CMU. Students use chat GPT personas for customer discovery and evaluating pricing decisions, bringing AI into entrepreneurial thinking and learning some very contemporary emerging topics to keep in mind, digital public infrastructure without the tragedy of the commons alternative data, AI's role in achieving the UN SDGs. These themes enrich our curriculum and broaden students' perspectives. There are a few supplementary slides here. I won't talk through all of them, but you can skim as they scroll. As we embrace gen ai, we must also nurture critical thinking and responsible AI practices for reading less, writing less, and doing less arithmetic. So all three Rs are arguably under attack, but we must think more deeply, more critically, know what to ask, how to verify. As Confucius said, I hear and I forget. I see, and I remember. I do and I understand. That's the heart of experiential learning and in today's fast moving world, as the Red Queen said, it takes all the running you can do to stay in the same place. To get somewhere else, you must run twice as fast. With AI as our ally, we're helping students ran faster and smarter. Thank you for your attention. I hope you found this useful.
